region_of_origin: East Asia (China, widely consumed across Southeast Asia including Singapore)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for boiled lotus root; SNOMED CT coverage of individual prepared vegetables is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled lotus root is approximately 100–120 g, equivalent to 4–6 slices, commonly served as a side dish or snack.

nutrient_source.title: Boiled Lotus Root — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for boiled lotus root (F14040030). All core macros and secondary nutrients are exact matches from this approved primary regional source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for boiled lotus root was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 53 is estimated based on the closest analogous starchy root vegetable (boiled taro/yam, GI ~53–55) from the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, AJCN). Lotus root has comparable starch structure, dietary fibre content (3.1 g/100g), and low sugar (0.5 g/100g), supporting a low-to-moderate GI estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 53 × 17.6 / 100 ≈ 9.3 (based on 110 g serving with 16.02 g carbohydrate/100g).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (45 mg/100g) and high potassium (363 mg/100g) with a favourable Na:K ratio supporting blood pressure reduction
hypertension.guidance: Boiled lotus root is suitable for individuals with hypertension; its low sodium content (45 mg/100g) and high potassium (363 mg/100g) align with DASH dietary principles, and patients should avoid adding soy sauce or salt-based seasonings post-cooking to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (16.02 g/100g) offset by dietary fibre (3.1 g/100g) and low sugar (0.5 g/100g), yielding an estimated low GI of approximately 53
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled lotus root is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions of 100–150 g per serving, as its dietary fibre moderates postprandial glycaemic response; total carbohydrate intake should be accounted for within the individual's daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat (0.07 g/100g), zero cholesterol (0.0 mg/100g), low sodium, high potassium, and meaningful dietary fibre (3.1 g/100g) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled lotus root is a heart-healthy vegetable choice for individuals with cardiovascular disease; its negligible fat, zero cholesterol, low sodium, and high potassium content support lipid and blood pressure management, and its dietary fibre contributes to LDL cholesterol reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Polyphenol content (catechins, procyanidins) and dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome health and anti-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled lotus root may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its polyphenol content with documented anti-inflammatory properties; it can be included freely as part of an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled lotus root (莲藕, Lian Ou) is the cooked rhizome of the aquatic plant Nelumbo nucifera, widely consumed across East and Southeast Asia including Singapore, China, Japan, and Korea. The root is sliced crosswise to reveal its characteristic lace-like pattern of air channels, producing white to off-white rounds with a tender yet slightly crunchy texture and a mild, subtly sweet flavour. Boiling is the simplest preparation method: the peeled and sliced root is simmered in water until tender, typically 15–20 minutes, and may be served plain, lightly seasoned, or incorporated into soups, stir-fries, and braised dishes. Nutritionally, boiled lotus root provides 66 kcal per 100 g, with 16.02 g carbohydrate, 1.58 g protein, and only 0.07 g fat, making it a low-fat, moderate-carbohydrate vegetable. Its dietary fibre content of 3.1 g/100g and low sugar (0.5 g/100g) moderate its glycaemic impact, with an estimated GI of approximately 53 (low category). The high potassium content (363 mg/100g) relative to its low sodium (45 mg/100g) makes it particularly suitable for individuals managing hypertension. Cholesterol is absent. Boiled lotus root is suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets. It is commonly consumed as a snack, side dish, or soup ingredient in Singaporean, Chinese, and broader Asian culinary traditions.
